aliases: North Indian Paneer Curry with Peas and Potatoes

english: North Indian Paneer Curry with Peas and Potatoes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Paneer Aloo Matar exists; mapped to broader curry dish class FOODON_03302571.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers paneer as a dairy product; no composite Paneer Aloo Matar entry exists. Mapped to paneer as the primary characterising ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite Indian curry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Aloo Matar is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of paneer cubes, potato pieces, and green peas in a spiced tomato-onion gravy, usually accompanied by bread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer curry with vegetables — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) entries: Paneer (cottage cheese), Potato boiled, Green peas cooked, and standard North Indian curry gravy components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Paneer Aloo Matar as a composite dish. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data: paneer (~40% by weight, ~265 kcal/100g, 18.3g protein, 20.8g fat), boiled potato (~30%, ~97 kcal/100g, 1.9g protein, 0.1g fat, 22.6g carb), cooked green peas (~15%, ~70 kcal/100g, 5.4g protein, 0.4g fat, 11.3g carb), and curry gravy base with oil (~15%, ~120 kcal/100g). Weighted composite per 100g of finished dish calculated accordingly. Sodium estimated from typical North Indian curry preparation using USDA SR Legacy salt/spice data as secondary reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry with paneer and potato — analogous estimate based on: (1) Boiled potato GI ~56 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (2) Green peas GI ~48 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) Paneer GI ~27 (dairy protein-fat matrix, University of Sydney GI Database); composite weighted GI ~52 for the finished dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Aloo Matar as a composite dish. GI estimated as weighted average of primary components: paneer (40% weight, GI ~27), boiled potato (30% weight, GI ~56), green peas (15% weight, GI ~48), and curry gravy base with oil (15% weight, GI ~15 due to fat/protein dampening). Weighted composite GI ≈ 52, classified as low (≤55). GL per 300g serving = (52 × 10.4 × 3) / 100 = 16.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from curry preparation and added salt
hypertension.guidance: A 300g restaurant serving of Paneer Aloo Matar contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) driven by potato content, partially offset by paneer protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of ~52 and glycemic load of ~16 per 300g serving place this dish in the moderate glycemic load range; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with a high-fibre bread or limit the potato proportion to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer and ghee/oil in curry preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 300g serving provides approximately 14.4g saturated fat, which approaches the daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association (13g for a 2000 kcal diet); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose preparations using minimal ghee and request smaller paneer portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Aloo Matar (also spelled Paneer Aloo Muttar or Mutter) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry combining three principal ingredients: paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese), aloo (potato), and matar (green peas), cooked together in a spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by first sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic in ghee or oil, then adding tomatoes and a blend of ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli powder to form a rich masala base. Cubed paneer, diced boiled potato, and green peas are folded into the gravy and simmered until the flavours meld. The texture is creamy and hearty, with the soft paneer absorbing the spiced sauce, the potato providing starchy bulk, and the peas adding sweetness and colour. The flavour profile is warm, mildly spiced, and savoury with a slight tang from tomato. Paneer Aloo Matar is a staple of North Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across India and the Indian diaspora, including Singapore, where it is served at North Indian restaurants in Boat Quay and South Bridge Road. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy food (~148 kcal/100g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~8.2g/100g) from paneer, moderate fat (~9.1g/100g, predominantly saturated from dairy), and a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~52) due to the fat-protein matrix of paneer tempering the potato's glycemic effect. It is a good source of calcium (~190 mg/100g) from paneer and provides iron, vitamin C from peas, and vitamin A from the spice base. Clinically, sodium content (~310 mg/100g) warrants attention for hypertension management, and saturated fat content is relevant for cardiovascular risk. The dish is naturally gluten-free and suitable for lacto-vegetarian and halal diets.

North Indian vegetarian curry with cottage cheese
meal_context: served with naan, roti, or basmati rice

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Paneer Aloo Matar as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for paneer, boiled potato, and cooked green peas with a standard curry gravy base. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish exists. Values should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN component data for paneer, potato, or peas, and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for these components. 4. GI of ~52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Aloo Matar was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 5. The food_name has been normalised to 'Paneer Aloo Matar' (standard spelling); the input variant 'Paneer Aloo Muttar' is retained as an alias. 6. Sodium estimate (~310 mg/100g) is based on typical North Indian restaurant curry preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component fat profiles; these are approximate.
